content.full_text:
A 96-05-065

May 12, 1976


Mr. Bruce Adams
Coopers and Lybrand
One Bush Street
San Francisco, California

Dear Mr. Adams:

This letter is to confirm our telephone conversation of May 10,
1976, concerning campaign disclosure requirements of the Political
Reform Act.

You stated the following facts: (1) The California Labor
Federation plans to send to its members, at a cost which will
exceed $500, a letter stating its position on Proposition 15;
(2) there has been no cooperation, mutual understanding or agree-
ment between the California Labor Federation and its political
arm, COPE, with regard to the mailing; (3) although the Federation
will pay for the mailing from membership dues, no portion of the
dues was earmarked for political purposes when collected; and
(4) the Federation has never used dues for political purposes.

Informed you that under these circumstances, the Federation will
become a committee under Government Code Section 82013(b) and
should file Form 460 to report the costs directly attributable to
the communication. Enclosed is a copy of 2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18225 which discusses the items which should be included
in computing the cost of the mailing.

If you have further questions, please contact our staff.

Sincerely,


NAN HAMBLETON
Special Compliance Representative

NH:mak
Enclosure

bcc: Jeanne

sections.conclusion: I informed you that under these circumstances, the Federation will become a committee under Government Code Section 82013(b) and should file Form 460 to report the costs directly attributable to the communication.
sections.question_synthetic: Does the California Labor Federation's planned mailing to members regarding Proposition 15, costing over $500, trigger campaign disclosure requirements under the Political Reform Act, and what reporting obligations arise?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The California Labor Federation must register as a committee under Government Code Section 82013(b) and file Form 460 to report the costs directly attributable to the Proposition 15 communication mailing.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document lacks formal QUESTION and CONCLUSION section headers but contains substantive Q&A in narrative format. Facts are clearly stated and advice is explicitly provided. Minor document quality issues (some characters appear corrupted or unclear) but core content is extractable.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does the California Labor Federation's planned mailing to members regarding Proposition 15, costing over $500, trigger campaign disclosure requirements under the Political Reform Act, and what reporting obligations arise?

CONCLUSION: I informed you that under these circumstances, the Federation will become a committee under Government Code Section 82013(b) and should file Form 460 to report the costs directly attributable to the communication.

You stated the following facts: (1) The California Labor Federation plans to send to its members, at a cost which will exceed $500, a letter stating its position on Proposition 15; (2) there has been no cooperation, mutual understanding or agree- ment between the California Labor Federation and its political arm, COPE, with regard to the mailing; (3) although the Federation will pay for the mailing from membership dues, no portion of the dues was earmarked for political purposes when collected; and (4) the Federation has never used dues for political purposes. Informed you that under these circumstances, the Federation will become a committee under Government Code Section 82013(b) and should file Form 460 to report the costs directly attributable to the communication. Enclosed is a copy of 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18225 which discusses the items which should be included in computing the cost of the mailing. If you have further questions, please contact our staff. embedding.summary: FPPC advises that the California Labor Federation's planned mailing to members regarding Proposition 15, exceeding $500 in cost, triggers campaign committee registration and Form 460 reporting requirements under the Political Reform Act. The FPPC provides guidance on what costs should be included in the disclosure calculation.
